Chem., 2017, DOI: 10.1039/C7NJ03880A. # Colorimetric Sensor of H2O2 Based on Co3O4–Montmorillonite Nanocomposites with the Peroxidase Activity Xixi Zhua, Wei Chena, Kaili Wua, Hongyu Lia, Min Fua, Qingyun Liu*a, Xiao Zhangb a College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, P. R. China b Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science & Technology, Qingdao 266042, China * Corresponding Author E-mail: qyliu@sdust.edu.cn; qyliusdu@163.com Tel.: +86 0532 86057757 Fax: +86 0532 80681197 ### Abstract Co3O4 nanoparticles deposited on montmorillonite (Co3O4-MMT NPs) were synthesized via a facile method and characterized by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Significantly, the as-prepared Co3O4-MMT NPs were demonstrated to possess an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity that can rapidly catalytic oxidize peroxidase substrate 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB·2HCl) by H2O2, accompanied with a visible color change (colorless to blue), which developed a sensitive colorimetric sensor for detecting H2O2. The catalytic reaction of Co3O4-MMT NPs followed the Michaelis-Menten kinetics equation, and the composites showed good affinity towards TMB. The proposed method can detect H2O2 in the range of 10-100 μM with a low detection limit (DL) of 8.7 μM. Fluorescent data revealed that the electron transfer among catalyst, TMB and H2O2 may contribute to the peroxidase-like activity of the Co3O4-MMT NPs. Finally, a colorimetric method for detecting H2O2 was proposed, and the robustness of the Co3O4-MMT NPs was pretty good. The sensor of H2O2 based on Co3O4-MMT NPs can be found to have great potential applications in the medical, food and environmental fields. **Key words:** Co3O4-MMT; nanocomposites; peroxidase; H2O2; colorimetric ### 1. Introduction As an important chemical substances, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been widely used in textile industry, chemical industry, paper industry, food industry, and so on. Moreover, H2O2 is a kind of intermediate in some metabolic reaction of human bodies, including glucose oxidation, trioxypurine oxidation, acetylcholine decomposition, etc. On this basis, it is possible to detect target object (glucose, trioxypurine, and acetylcholine) though measuring the concentration of H2O2 indirectly. In addition, H2O2 also exists in some food, seawater and waste water. Thus, it is necessary to develop a convenient and facile method to determine the concentration of these micromolecules, such as H2O2 and acetylcholine [1-3]. Compared with traditional methods, such as electrochemistry [4], chemiluminescence [5], liquid chromatography [6], fluorescence [7] and bioelectrochemistry [8], the colorimetric assay attracts more attention due to its low-cost, timesaving, sensitivity and pretty simplicity [9]. Colorimetric detection technologies provide a valuable set of approaches suitable in routine tests for environmental monitoring and biomedical diagnosis purposes. As a natural peroxidases, horseradish peroxidase (HRP) can catalyze the oxidation of some substrates (ascorbate, ferrocyanide, catochrome C and the leuco form of many dyes, etc.) in the presence of H2O2 [10]. Thus, HRP is the most common enzyme for H2O2 sensing. However, the application of HRP has been limited due to the complicated process of preparation and purification, as well as easy inactivation under extreme conditions, including basic medium, strong acidic and high temperature [11]. It is amusing that the magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles possess the peroxidase-like activity, which could be used as mimic enzymes to replace HRP [12] Since then, various nanomaterials were investigated as peroxidase, such as Co3O4 NPs [13], TCPP-NiO NPs [14], Fe–Co NPs [15], CNPs [16], etc. As we know, nanoparticles are tend to aggregate, due to the relatively smaller particle size with high interface energy, resulting in the decrease of catalytic activity to some extent. Thus, some supports are necessary to prepare the nanocomposite, in order to maintain the catalytic activity of small nanoparticles. Montmorillonite (MMT) is one kind of phyllosilicate minerals composed by one layer of aluminum-oxygen octahedron and two layers of silicon-oxygen tetrahedron, and it has been widely applied in separation, catalysis, optical and electrical devices due to layered structure, large specific area and high cation exchange capacity [17]. There are many reports on MMT based materials, included inorganic materials and organic materials, such as ZnO-MMT, CeO2-MMT, CuS-MMT, and Polyethylene-MMT etc [18-24]. From these reports, it can be found that MMT plays a key role in the dispersion of other components, and then prevents the aggregation of the nanoparticles. With these ideas in our mind, the combination of Co3O4 and MMT is attractive to construct an efficient nanocomposite as enzyme mimic. So far as we know, there is no relevant report about Co3O4-MMT NPs applied in H2O2 detection. Herein, Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal route and investigated as efficient and novel peroxidase mimics. Co3O4 nanoparticles were homogeneously distributed on the MMT sheets. Co3O4-MMT NPs possessed the catalytic activity of peroxidase and weak oxidase, and showed typical Michealis-Menten kinetics and good affinity toward TMB. The peroxidase-like activity of the Co3O4-MMT NPs may originate from the electron transfer among catalyst, TMB and H2O2. The fluorescent technology was employed to verify the catalytic activities of Co3O4-MMT NPs and to support the mechanism analysis. Based on the peroxidase activity, sensitive colorimetric sensors of H2O2 was designed. ### 2. Experimental section #### 2.1 Reagents Co(NO3)2·6H2O, acetic acid (HAc), hydrogen peroxide (30% wt%, H2O2) and sodium acetate (NaAc)were obtained from Beijingchemical Reagent Factory (Beijing, China). 3,3,5,5-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and Montmorillonite (MMT) were obtained from Solarbio (Tianjin, China). Terephthalic acid (TA) was obtained from Chengdu cologne chemical reagent factory. The chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade. All solutions were prepared with De-ionized water. #### 2.2 Characterization The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained using a Rigaku D/Max2500PC diffractometer with graphite monochromatized Cu K$\alpha$ radiation. The elemental compositions of the composites were identified using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The morphology and shape of the nanocomposites were imaged by the transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a Hitachi UH4150 spectrophotometer (Japan). Fluorometric measurement was carried out by a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). #### 2.3 Preparation of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites were prepared as follows: Firstly, 0.3000g MMT was dissolved in 13mL alcohol aqueous solution (H2O2:C2H5OH=1:1) under stirring violently for 18h to form a homogeneous suspension. Secondly, 0.2910g of the Co(NO3)2 (1mmol) were added into the above suspension under vigorous stirring for another 2h. The resulting solution was assigned as solution A. Thirdly, 1.25 ml ammonium hydroxide was cautiously dropwise added into the solution A and stirred for 1h. Subsequently, the above solution was sealed in an autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 8h. Finally, the products were collected by centrifugation, washed with distilled water for three times and subsequently ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C for 8 h in air. #### 2.4 Peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites The peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites was investigated via the results of catalytic oxidizing the substrate TMB by H2O2. In a typical procedure, 0.2 mL of TMB (1 mM) in sodium acetate buffer solution (pH=4.0) was dealt with different reaction systems, listed in Table 1. The total reaction volume was 2 mL. The absorption spectra of different experimental systems were measured by recording the absorption spectra in wavelength mode (500-800nm). **Table 1** Different reaction systems.
Number Different reaction systems
a 0.2 mL Co3O4-MMT (0.3 mg mL−1) + 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
b 0.2 mL Co3O4-MMT (0.3 mg mL−1)
c 0.2 mL MMT (0.3 mg mL−1) + 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
d 0.2 mL Co3O4 NPs (0.3 mg mL−1) + 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
e 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
f Nothing
#### 2.5 Kinetics measurements The kinetic measurement of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites was executed on a UV-vis absorbance spectrophotometer under time-scan mode. The experiment process was as follows: 0.3 mg mL−1 Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites as enzyme mimics in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH=4.0) with 0.2 mL of TMB (1mM) and 0.2 mL of H2O2 (10–1000 mM) as a substrate, or 0.2 mL of H2O2 (0.25 M) and 0.2 mL of TMB (0.1–1 mM) as a substrate, and keep total reaction volume was 2mL. The Michaelis–Menten constant (Km) could be obtained according to the Lineweaver-Burk plot: $1/v = K_m/V_{max} (1/[S] + 1/K_m)$, where v, Vmax, [S] and Km is represent the initial velocity, the maximal reaction velocity, the concentration of the substrate, and the Michaelis constant, respectively [25]. #### 2.6 Detection of H2O2 using Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites The experiments of colorimetric detection of H2O2 with Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites as catalysts were performed as follows: using TMB (0.2 mL, 1 mM) as peroxidase substrate in the presence of H2O2 (0.2 mL) and 0.2 mL Co3O4-MMT NPs (0.3 mg·mL−1), together with 1.4 mL sodium acetate buffer solution (pH=4.0). The catalytic reaction of TMB oxidized by H2O2 in the presence of Co3O4-MMT NPs was investigated according to the absorbance of the resulting solution. ### 3. Results and discussion #### 3.1 Characterization ![XRD spectra](XRD_spectra.png) **Fig. 1.** The XRD spectra of MMT (a), Co3O4 NPs (b) and Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites (c), respectively. The XRD patterns of MMT, Co3O4, and Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites were shown in Fig.1. Diffraction peak at 7.21° in Fig.1a was the characteristic peak of MMT correspond to (001) plane. From fig.1b, diffraction peaks at 19.04°, 31.32°, 36.89°, and 44.87° corresponded to the (1 1 1), (2 2 0), (3 1 1) and (4 0 0) planes) respectively, which agreed well with reported Co3O4 system (JCPDS card no.42-1467) [26], thus demonstrating the existence of the crystalline Co3O4. From the Fig.1c, besides the above characteristic peaks of Co3O4, the peak appears at 7.89° can be indexed as (001) plane of MMT, suggesting that Co3O4 nanoparticles were deposited on the surface of MMT, which can be observed in the subsequent TEM images. ![TEM images](TEM_images.png) **Fig. 2.** TEM images of MMT (A), Co3O4 NPs (B) and Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites (C), respectively. The morphologies of the MMT, Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites were imaged by TEM, shown in Fig. 2. It can be seen from Fig. 2b that Co3O4 nanoparticles without MMT as support easily aggregated. However, as shown in Fig. 2c, the Co3O4 nanoparticles with ca. 10-20 nm in size were dispersed well on the surface of MMT sheets, suggesting that MMT was an ideal support for the growth of Co3O4 nanoparticles. In order to determine the influence of the reaction time on the preparation of nanocomposites, we have carried out the experiments for different reaction time (2h, 4h, 6h, 8h, 10h). And the obtained nanocomposites were characterized by XRD and SEM, shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 (supporting information), respectively. From the XRD patterns, it can be seen that Co3O4 nanoparticles have been formed after reaction for 2h. The diffraction peaks at 19.04°, 31.32°, 36.89°, and 44.87° agreed well with that of peak of Co3O4 (JCPDS card no.42-1467). Moreover, from the XRD data, it can be found that the longer the experimental time, the more the intensity of the peaks of Co3O4 nanoparticles. It can be found that when experiments have carried out for 8 h, the intensity of diffraction peaks of Co3O4 nanoparticles have reached maximum. However, it can be also found that the XRD peaks of MMT is hardly any changed. From SEM images shown in Fig S2 (supporting information), it can be seen that nanocomposites, which have been prepared under the condition of reaction for 8 h, dispersed well on the surface of MMT. Nevertheless, when the reaction have sustained for 10 h, it can be found that Co3O4 nanoparticles have agglomerated on the surface of MMT to some extent. On the basis of the results described above, it can be indicated that Montmorillonite provide a good supporter for Co3O4 growing, but the influence of Montmorillonite on the XRD of the Co3O4 is very limited. ![EDS spectra](EDS_spectra.png) **Fig. 3.** Energy-dispersive X-ray spectra of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites The chemical components of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites were further confirmed by EDS, shown in Fig. 3. It can be observed that there are two obvious Co and O peaks, strongly suggesting that Co3O4 successfully anchored onto MMT materials. The reason why the content of O was more than that of Co was that the montmorillonite also had lots of O elements. In addition, the peak of Si in the spectrum was attributed to montmorillonite. #### 3.2 Peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites The peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites was investigated via the results of catalytic oxidizing TMB by H2O2. TMB was oxidized into an intermediate product with an obvious color change from colorless to blue corresponding to a strong absorption band at 652 nm, and this process could be observed by the naked eyes. From Fig. 4, it can be seen that the absorbance of system (a) is higher than that of other systems, indicating that Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites possessed the enhanced the peroxidase catalytic activity. It also can be found that Co3O4 nanoparticles (b) possessed the peroxidase activity, which is in accordance with the previous report [13], weaker than that of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites, suggesting that MMT as a good supporter played an important role in dispersion of Co3O4 nanoparticles. That is to say, MMT increases the dispersity of Co3O4 nanoparticles, and the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites had more active sites with substrate than that of sole Co3O4 nanoparticles. By contrast, the experimental system without Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites (d) exhibited a negligible color change. It can be concluded from system (e) that sole MMT was not demonstrated the peroxidase activity. System (f) shows no obvious color change, indicating that Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites are required for the catalytic oxidation reaction. Interestingly, system (c) demonstrated a wathet blue change, indicating the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites possessed weak oxidase-like activity. Overall, Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites exhibited peroxidase- and oxidase-like catalytic activity under identical conditions. It is the di-enzymes effects that accelerate the oxidation of TMB and result in the rapid response for H2O2. Subsequently, we emphasized to study the peroxidase-like properties of Co3O4-MMT, because the oxidase-like activity is relatively weak. ![Absorbance spectra](Absorbance_spectra.png) **Fig. 4.** (a) TMB + Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites + H2O2, (b) TMB + Co3O4 + H2O2 (c) TMB + Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites, (d) Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites + H2O2, (e) TMB +MMT+ H2O2, (f) TMB + H2O2, and (j) TMB, respectively. Each of systems was measured by monitoring the absorbance data from 500 to 800 nm. The catalytic role of Co3O4-MMT on ABTS and pyrogallol were assessed and compared with TMB as shown in fig. S3. In the same reaction time, the ABTS and pyrogallol shows weak color changes. In contrast, the obvious blue change of the TMB is obtained. #### 3.3 Experimental condition optimization Similar to natural peroxidases and the previous reported nanomaterial-based peroxidase mimics [27-29], the catalytic activity of Co3O4-MMT NPs depends on pH and temperature, respectively. Changes of the pH (1.7-9.0) and the temperature (20 - 60°C) were executed to evaluate the influences of pH and temperature on catalytic activity of Co3O4-MMT NPs, respectively. As shown in Fig.5a, the catalytic activity of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites first increased untill pH=4.0 and then declined with pH value increases. Thus, the optimal pH was approximately 4.0, revealing that this reaction occurred easily in weak acidic solutions, which was consistent with the previous reports [14, 20]. Fig.5b shows temperature dependence of catalytic activity. The highest catalytic activity was obtained at approximately 40°C, indicating that the optimal temperature was 40°C, which was consistent with porphyrin functionalized ZnS nanoparticles [30]. ![pH and temperature influences](pH_temp_influences.png) **Fig. 5.** Influences of (A) pH and (B) temperature on the catalytic oxidation of 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 and Co3O4-MMT NPs. The maximum point in each curve (A–B) was set as 100%. #### 3.4 Kinetic studies of the peroxidase-like activity of the Co3O4-MMT NPs It is known that the catalytic oxidation of TMB by many enzymes mimetics follows the Michaelis-Menten behaviors. Michaelis-Menten equation ($v = V_{max}S/(K_m + S)$) as one of the earliest descriptions of the enzymes action, can reveal the relationship between substrate concentration (S) and initial rate (v). Series of assays were carried out with the change of one substrate concentration, and kept the other conditions fixed at the same time, so as to further inveatigate the peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT NPs. Typical Michaels-Menten curves of TMB and H2O2 were obtained by plotting concentration against the initial reaction rate, shown in Fig. 6. ![Kinetic analysis](Kinetic_analysis.png) **Fig. 6.** Steady-state kinetic analysis utilizing the Michaelis-Menten model (A, B) and Lineweaver–Burk double-reciprocal model (C, D) for Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites. The concentration of H2O2 was 0.25 M while TMB concentration was altered (A, C). In turn, the concentration of TMB was 1 mM and H2O2 concentration was varied (B, D). Michaelis constant (Km) is often related to the affinity between the substrate and the catalyst obtained by the Lineweaver-Burk (L-B) plot. The smaller the value of Km, the stronger the affinity between the substrate and the catalysts. Comparison of maximum reaction rate (Vmax) and the apparent Michealis constant (Km) between Co3O4-MMT NPs and HRP were illustrated in Table 2. It can be seen that the Km value for Co3O4-MMT NPs with TMB as the substrate is calculated to be 0.002163 mM, which was 20 times lower than that for HRP enzyme, indicating that the stronger affinity of Co3O4-MMT NPs towards TMB than that of HRP. However, the Km value of Co3O4-MMT NPs with H2O2 as substrate is 20.492 mM, higher than that of HRP. **Table 2** Comparison of the apparent Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and the maximum reaction velocity (Vmax).

#### 3.5 Mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT NPs In order to figure out the catalytic mechanism of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites, a series of experiments were carried out. In these experiments, terephthalic acid was employed as a fluorescence probe to replace TMB and estimate the effects of the Co3O4-MMT NPs on [·OH] radicals. Terephthalic acid easily captured [·OH] radicals to form 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid which can generate high fluorescent intensity [33]. The typical assay was performed using 10mM H2O2, 0.5mM terephthalic acid and the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites with different concentration were incubated in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH=4.0, 100 mM) at 40 °C for 40 min. The fluorescent results were displayed in Fig. 7. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the amount of the Co3O4-MMT NPs increased, which was inconsistent with that of some previous works [14, 30]. In other words, the higher concentration of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites, the less [·OH] radicals produced, suggesting that the composites inhibit the production of [·OH] radicals. According to the relevant publications [34-35], this kind of efficient catalytic performance may originate from the electron transfer among catalyst, TMB and H2O2. On the basis of experimental results and relevant reports, we proposed the catalytic mechanism of the Co3O4-MMT composites, illustrated in Scheme 1. The higher catalytic activity of Co3O4-MMT composites may be explained due to transfer of electrons from the top of the valence band of Co3O4-MMT composites to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of H2O2. Moreover, the amino groups of TMB donate lone-pair electrons to Co3O4-MMT composites, which confers an increase in electron density and mobility in Co3O4-MMT composites. This transfer of charges increases the electro-chemical potential from the LUMO of H2O2 and therefore helps the electron transfer process from Co3O4-MMT composites to H2O2 [36]. ![Fluorescence intensity](Fluorescence_intensity.png) **Fig. 7.** The influence of the Co3O4-MMT NPs on the generation of hydroxyl radical with terephthalic acid (TA) as a fluorescent probe. Reaction conditions: TA (0.35 mM), H2O2 (0.25 M), and the Co3O4-MMT NPs with different concentration (0–700 g mL-1) as the curves indicated (a–h) were incubated in acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 100 mM) at 40 °C for 40 min. ![Scheme 1](Scheme_1.png) **Scheme 1.** Schematic illustration of colorimetric detection of H2O2 catalyzed by using Co3O4-MMT NPs. #### 3.6 Detection of H2O2 and using Co3O4-MMT NPs According to the above results, it can be inferred that the Co3O4-MMT NPs possessed intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, which can catalytic oxidize TMB by H2O2 to obtain the blue oxTMB. Therefore, a simple colorimetric method for detecting H2O2 was developed. Under the optimum experimental conditions, the absorbance at 652 nm versus concentrations of H2O2 response curve was plotted in Fig. 8A. The absorbance gradually increased with the increasing of H2O2 concentration ranged from 2 to 900 μM. The corresponding linear calibration plot was shown in Fig. 8b, revealing a good linear relationship in the range of 10-100 μM with a detection limit of 8.7μM, slightly lower than that of pure Co3O4 NPs (10 μM) [13]. Furthermore, the visualized variation could be observed by the naked eye, which represents the blue becomes darker and darker with the increasing concentration of the H2O2, as shown in the inset of Fig. 8b. ![Dose-response curve](Dose_response_curve.png) **Fig. 8.** (A) A dose-response curve depending of the absorbance at 652 nm on the concentration of H2O2 from 2μM to 900 μM. (B) The corresponding linear calibration plot, in which, error bars represent the standard error originated from three repeated measurements. Inset: photographs of colored products at different concentration of H2O2. **Table 3** Comparison of the response of other H2O2 sensors.

#### 3.7 Application to real sample analysis In modern society, contact lenses has been increasingly used, and in the contact lens industry, H2O2 is often adopted in the disinfection process [37-38]. However, H2O2 is harmful to human eyes because of its oxidatice properties. Therefore, it is important to determine the content of H2O2 in the contact lens. As mentioned above, colorimetric method for detecting H2O2 was proposed based on Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites, and a series of assays were performed to confirm the content of H2O2 in the contact lens based on our proposed method under the above optimal conditions following. Moreover, the standard addition method was adopted in these assays under the same conditions for three times. The lens care solutions were spiked with different concentration of H2O2, and the results were listed in Table 4. There are no obvious differences between spiked and values obtained by the calibration curve (Fig. 8b). Moreover, the recovery of H2O2 in four lens care solution samples is in the range of 102 - 106.7% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were as low as 0.39%, indicating that this colorimetric H2O2 sensor based on our proposed method is feasible in real simple analysis. **Table 4** Determination of H2O2 in lens care solution in the presence of Co3O4-MMT NPs

#### 3.8 Robustness of peroxidase activity of Co3O4-MMT NPs As we know, the stability of the catalyst is of great importance for the following application. The temperature and pH are the most common impact factors on the structure and catalytic activity of catalysts. According to the reference [12], the catalytic activity of the HRP dramatically decreased after being treated at different temperatures and pH values for 2 hours. Based on the similar method, the stability of Co3O4-MMT NPs were studied in this paper. The activity of the Co3O4-MMT NPs were measured under optimum conditions (pH 4.0, 40 °C) after being incubated at different pH values (2-10) and temperatures (0-80 °C) for 2 h, shown in Fig. 9 and Fig. 10, respectively. From the data, it can be seen that there are no obvious changes of Co3O4-MMT activity after incubating in different pH values and temperatures, and the lowest relative activitiy are exceed 90%, indicating that the Co3O4-MMT NPs possesses good stability under a widely range of pH values form 1 to 10 and temperatures from 0 to 80 °C, respectively. ![pH stability](pH_stability.png) **Fig. 9.** Relative catalytic activity of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites after incubation at a range of values of pH (1.0–10.0) for 2 h. ![Temperature stability](Temp_stability.png) **Fig. 10.** Relative catalytic activity of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites after incubation at a range of temperatures (0–80 °C) for 2 h. Furthermore, the reusability of the catalysts is also very crucial. In order to figure out the reusability of Co3O4-MMT NPs, a series of experiments were performed to examine the catalytic activity in different cycles. From Fig. 14, it can be concluded that only a slightly decline of relative activity was observed after 5 cycles. In conclusion, the robustness of the Co3O4-MMT NPs is pretty good and it can be used in the practical applications under various fields. ![Reusability](Reusability.png) **Fig. 11.** Relative catalytic activity of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites in five successive recycling catalysis. ### 4. Conclusion In summary, this work reported a facile method to synthesize Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites by one-step hydrothermal route, and this nanoparticles were demonstrated to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity which could catalytic oxidize TMB by H2O2 accompanied with visible color change by naked eyes. The Co3O4-MMT NPs exhibited higher catalytic activity than pure MMT and the catalytic capacity was strongly dependent on temperature and pH. The results showed that the catalysis followed typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics and had a strong affinity to TMB. This kind of efficient catalytic performance may derive from the electron transfer among catalyst, TMB and H2O2. Based on the results described above, a simple, colorimetric and visual method for detecting H2O2 was established, which had a promising application in food detection, biochemical analysis and environmental monitoring. ![Final Schematic](Final_Schematic.png) Schematic illustration of colorimetric detection of H2O2 catalyzed by using Co3O4-MMT NPs.

Synthesis, Magnetic Characterization, and Sensing Applications of Novel Dextran-Coated Iron Oxide Nanorods Sudip Nath,† Charalambos Kaittanis,†,‡ Vasanth Ramachandran,§ Naresh S. Dalal,§ and J. Manuel Perez*,†,‡,|| Nanoscience Technology Center, Burnett School of Biomedical Sciences, College of Medicine, and Department of Chemistry, University of Central Florida, Orlando, Florida 32826, and Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Florida State University, Tallahassee, FL 32306 Received November 24, 2008. Revised Manuscript Received January 31, 2009 Monodisperse, water-soluble dextran-coated iron oxide (Fe3O4) nanorods were synthesized using a facile and scalable approach. Our room temperature method involves the mixing of an acidic solution of iron salts with a basic solution of ammonium hydroxide to facilitate initial formation of iron oxide crystals. The stability, crystallinity, and shape of these nanorods depends on the time of addition of the dextran, as well as the degree of purity of the polymer. The as-synthesized nanorods exhibit unique magnetic properties, including superparamagnetic behavior and high spin–spin water relaxivity (R2). Additionally, they possess enhanced peroxidase-like activity when compared to those reported in the literature for spherical iron oxide nanoparticles. Thus, this high yield synthetic method for polymer-coated iron oxide nanorods will expedite their use in applications from magnetic sensors, devices, and nanocomposites with magnetic and catalytic properties. ### Introduction Iron oxide-based magnetic nanoparticles have been widely used in a variety of biomedical applications such as magnetic separation,1 magnetic resonance imaging,2 hyperthermia,3,4 magnetically guided drug delivery,5,6 tissue repair,3 and molecular diagnostics.7–9 For most applications, a polymeric coating is needed to improve the nanoparticles’ aqueous stability, biocompatibility, and conjugation properties. For instance, dextran-coated iron oxide nanoparticles have been successfully used as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents because of their strong ability to dephase water protons in surrounding tissue, which results in a decrease in the MRI signal.10 In addition, the dextran coating can be cross-linked and functionalized with amino groups to facilitate the conjugation of targeting ligands for MRI and in vitro diagnostics applications.11 Current synthetic procedures for dextran-coated iron oxide nanoparticles involve the formation of the iron oxide core in the presence of dextran, as stabilizer and capping agent.12 Under these in situ conditions, the nature, quality, and amount of the polymer modulate the nucleation, growth, and size of the newly formed iron oxide nanocrystal. A common characteristic of most reported in situ dextran-coated iron oxide nanoparticles synthetic procedures is the formation of nanoparticles with a spherical iron oxide core. Research efforts have been geared toward the production of small, uniform and highly dispersed spherical nanocrystals. Only recently, the importance of the nanoparticles’ shape has been recognized, in particular one-dimensional (1D) structures such as nanorods and nanotubes, because they exhibit unique properties that are different from their corresponding zero dimensional counterparts (0D or spherical nanocrystals). Particularly in the case of iron oxide, 1D nanorods have been found to exhibit interesting magnetic properties because of their shape anisotropy, such as higher blocking temperatures and larger magnetization coercivity, compared to their 0D counterparts.13–16 However, their wide application in biomedical research has been hampered by difficult procedures, use of toxic reagents,17 and poor yields. For instance, current methods for iron oxide nanorods involve hydrothermal,18,19 sol–gel,20 and high-temperature procedures,17,21 among others. Therefore, water-based synthetic procedures for iron oxide nanorods that are simple, economical, low temperature, and high yield are in high demand. In particular, synthetic methods that yield water-soluble and stable polymer-coated nanorods would be ideal for studies geared toward the development of magnetic biosensors and magnetic devices. Here we report a facile, high-yield, room-temperature, and water-based synthetic protocol that yields disperse dextran-coated iron oxide nanorods (DIONrods). Our synthetic procedure differs from those previously reported methods for dextran-coated iron oxide nanoparticles in that the dextran is not present during the initial nucleation process. Instead, the dextran is added at a later stage. This “stepwise” process, as opposed to the in situ process, allows the formation of stable, disperse, and highly crystalline iron oxide nanorods that exhibit supermagnetic behavior and improved high water relaxivity. First, we used these nanorods as magnetic nanosensors for the sensitive and fast detection of a particular bacterium in milk, achieving a detection limit of 6 cfu (colony forming units) in just 5 min. Next, the peroxidase-like activity of the as-synthesized rods was also evaluated, demonstrating that our DIONrods possess enhanced peroxidase activity as compared to their spherical counterparts. Finally, studies using dextrans with various degrees of purity revealed that rod formation is favored when a highly pure polymer is used. ### Materials and Methods **Materials.** Iron salts, FeCl2 · 4H2O, and FeCl3 · 6H2O were purchased from Sigma. Dextran (10 kDa) was received from Amersham Biosciences and Sigma. Sulfo-N-hydroxysuccinimide-LC-Biotin, disuccinimidyl suberate (DSS) and N-Succinimidyl 3-(2-pyridyldithio)-propionate (SPDP) were purchased from Pierce. The peroxidase-sensitive dye 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), protein G, and concanavalin A were received from Sigma. The anti-MAP antibody and MAP bacteria were kindly supplied by Dr Saleh Naser from the Microbiology and Molecular Biology Department, University of Central Florida. **Synthesis of Iron Oxide Nanoparticles.** An acidic solution of iron salts containing 0.203 g of FeCl2 · 4H2O and 0.488 g of FeCl3 · 6H2O in HCl solution (88.7 μL of 12 N HCl in 2 mL of water) was mixed with ammonium hydroxide solution (830 μL of NH4OH in 15 mL of DI water) under stirring conditions using a digital vortex meter. The reaction mixture turned black instantaneously and after a few seconds (θ) of stirring, a solution of dextran (5 g in 10 mL of DI water) was added to yield dextran-coated iron oxide nanoparticles. The resultant solution continued to be stirred for 1 h and was then centrifuged at 13 000 rpm for 30 min to get rid of large particles. The supernatant was collected, washed several times with distilled water, and concentrated through an Amicon 8200 cell (Millipore ultrafiltration membrane YM - 30 k). This results in dextran-coated iron oxide nanorods (DIONrods). To prepare aminated nanoparticles, 3 mg (i.e., 3 mL of solution containing ~1 mg of iron/mL) of DIONrod suspension in water was treated with 5 mL of 0.5 M NaOH and 200 μL of epicholohydrin. The mixture was stirred vigorously at room temperature for 8 h. Afterward, 850 μL of 30% ammonia was added and stirred for 12 h to complete the amination procedure. The free epichlorohydrin was removed by washing the solution repeatedly with distilled water using Amicon cell. The resulting aminated DIONrods are highly stable in water and phosphate buffer saline for months. **Characterization.** The particles were characterized using HRTEM (FEI TECNAI F30), XPS (Physical Electronics 5400 ESCA) and XRD (Rigaku D/MAX XRD) techniques. T2 was measured using a Bruker Minispec mq20 NMR analyzer operating at 0.47 T at 37 °C. A Quantum Design SQUID magnetic property measurement system (MPMS XL) was used to perform all the magnetic measurements on the Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Zero-field-cooled (ZFC) and field-cooled (FC) measurements of dc magnetic susceptibility were done using a 200 G field, by varying the temperature from 1.8 to 250 K. In the ZFC process, the sample was first cooled under zero external magnetic field, followed by the application of $H = 200$ G, whereas in the FC process, the sample was cooled in the presence of magnetic field $H = 200$ G, before sweeping the temperature. Hysteresis loops were measured at temperatures 5, 100, and 200 K with the magnetic field varying from −2 to 2 T. Zero field ac magnetic susceptibility as a function of temperature was measured at frequencies ranging from 0.01 Hz to 1 kHz, using a drive field of 5 G. The amount of iron present per ml of solution was determined using acid digestion method. The quantification of amines present on the surface of the aminated DIONrods was done as described.11 **Synthesis of MAP Nanosensors and Detection of MAP Bacteria in Milk.** The aminated DIONrods were centrifuged at 13 000 rpm and the pellet was redispersed in DMSO. The nanorods suspension in DMSO (650 μL, 0.1 mg Fe/mL) was then incubated with 6.89 mg of disuccinimidyl suberate (DSS) for 12 h at room temperature. The reaction mixture was then centrifuged and the pellet was washed and redispersed in PBS. Finally, the modified nanorods were incubated with protein G (1 mg/ml) and kept for 12 h at 4 °C. The excess protein G was removed by magnetic separation using a MACS magnetic column and the amount of protein G was assessed by protein assay as described.9,22 The resulting protein G-conjugated nanorods (protein G DIONrods) were treated with anti-MAP-antibody (10 μL) and incubated overnight in order to facilitate a stable protein G–anti-MAP antibody association. The average number of antibodies conjugated per nanoparticle was assessed as previously described.9,11,22 The resulting anti-MAP DIONrods (200 μL) were used as magnetic nanosensors to detect MAP bacteria in milk (20 μL) and the changes in T2 were monitored using a 0.47T magnetic relaxometer (MiniSpec, Bruker) to detect the bacteria present in the sample. **Kinetic Assay of DIONrods Peroxidase Activity.** The peroxidase activity of the iron oxide nanoparticles was evaluated via steady-state kinetic assays at room temperature. Specifically, serial dilutions of TMB were prepared in citrate buffer (pH 4), and 100 μL aliquots were dispensed in a 96-well plate. Subsequently, aliquots of either the corresponding DIONrod preparations, having equal iron concentrations (2 μg per mL), were added to the wells. After addition of 25 μL of H2O2 (30%), each well’s absorbance was recorded at 652 nm, using a Synergy HT microtiter plate reader (Biotek). The kinetic parameters were determined via the Michaelis–Menten equation: $v = V_{max} [S]/(K_m + [S])$, where $v$ is the initial rate of reaction, $V_{max}$ is the maximum rate of reaction, $[S]$ is the substrate’s concentration, and $K_m$ is the Michaelis constant. ### Results and Discussions In our first set of experiments, we optimized the synthetic protocol, including the order of addition (in situ vs stepwise), and the time of addition of the dextran polymer. Our water-based synthetic protocol involves an acid–base reaction between an acidic solution of iron salts and a basic solution of ammonium hydroxide. Upon mixing, the resulting solution becomes alkaline (pH 9.0), facilitating the formation of iron oxide nanocrystals. This initial formation of nanocrystals can occur either in the presence (in situ) or absence (stepwise) of dextran. Because most synthetic procedures that afford stable and monodisperse nanoparticles use an in situ approach, we opted to follow this approach first. In these experiments, a mixture of iron salts (FeCl3 · 6H2O and FeCl2 · 4H2O) was dissolved in an aqueous solution of HCl, whereas a dextran solution was prepared in aqueous ammonia solution and placed on a digital vortex meter. Next, the acidic iron salt solution was poured into the ammonia solution of dextran under vigorous stirring condition. Following this protocol, we obtained poorly crystalline, spherical nanocrystals of 20 ± 5 nm in diameter and poor R2 relaxivity (<1 mM−1 s−1). This poor relaxivity contrasts with the relaxivity obtained with other published in situ procedures where relaxivity values between 60 and 100 mM−1 s−1 are obtained. We then investigated if a stepwise approach will result in larger iron oxide nanocrystals and an improvement in R2 relaxivity. In this approach, a dextran solution was added at a particular time (θ) after initiating the iron oxide nanocrystal’s nucleation process. In initial optimization experiments, we measured T2 relaxivity (R2) and obtained TEM images of a series of dextran coated iron oxide nanoparticles prepared after adding dextran at different (θ) times (1, 10, 30, and 60 s). Following this approach, we obtained nanorods for which their size and R2 relaxivity can be fine-tuned by varying the time of dextran’s addition (θ) (Table 1). No significant difference was observed in the crystallinity and magnetic properties (particularly R2) of the nanorods synthesized at θ ≥ 30 s. However, the yield of the iron oxide nanorods was greatly reduced under these conditions (θ ≥ 30 s), based on measurements of the iron concentrations of the nanorods’ suspension.11,23 The observed low yield at θ ≥ 30 s could be due to the fact that a higher number of larger (micrometer-size) crystals are formed when the stabilizing agent (dextran) is added at a later time after initiation of the nucleation process. These micrometer-size particles tend to precipitate easily and are removed from suspension during the purification process. Because the most optimal preparation was obtained when dextran was added 30 s after initiating the iron oxide nucleation, we then adopted these conditions in our nanorods synthesis. TEM studies (Figure 1a) of the DIONrods preparation (R2 = 300 mM−1 s−1) reveal monodisperse rod-shaped iron oxide crystals, with an average length of 310 ± 10 nm and width of 135 ± 5 nm, for an aspect ratio of 2.3. Corresponding HRTEM (Figure 1b) analysis and selected area electron diffraction (inset of Figure 1b) confirm the formation of highly crystalline nanocrystals. X-ray diffraction pattern of DIONrods Figure 1c indicates eight distinct peaks at 18.2, 30.1, 35.6, 37, 44.2, 53.6, 57, and 62.5°, accounting for crystal planes 111, 200, 311, 222, 400, 422, 511, and 440. These X-ray diffraction data correspond to the formation of magnetite (Fe3O4) nanocrystals.24 Meanwhile, XPS was performed to determine the oxidation state of the iron present. Two distinct peaks at 715 and 728 eV representing Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2 orbital electrons, as shown in Figure 1d, confirmed the formation of magnetite in the solution.25 Using SQUID magnetometry, we studied the magnetic properties of the DIONrods.26 First, hysteresis loops measured at three different temperatures (Figure 2a) demonstrated a coercivity of 500 ± 10 G at 5 K that disappeared at 100 and 200 K, which is typical of superparamagnetic behavior. Zero-field-cooled (ZFC) and field-cooled (FC) dc susceptibility studies (Figure 2b) show that the ZFC magnetic moment increased as the temperature increased, reaching a maximum at 28 K (the blocking temperature, $T_B$); it then decreased with a further increase in temperature. In the FC process, above the blocking temperature ($T_B$), the data followed the ZFC curve but deviated from ZFC curve below $T_B$, showing a slow increase in the moment with decreasing temperature. The maximum found in the ZFC curve (at $T_B$) is where a maximum number of particles exhibit superparamagnetic behavior. Below $T_B$, the relaxation times of the particles are longer than the experimental measurement time; hence the particles acquire a blocked state. In the ac susceptibility, both real and imaginary components $\chi'(T)$ and $\chi''(T)$, at different frequencies ranging from 1 Hz to 1kHz exhibited a frequency-dependent maximum (panels c and d in Figure 2), which shifted to higher temperatures with increasing frequency. This may be due to either spin glass or superparamagnetic behavior. To clearly distinguish between these two behaviors, we calculated the Mydosh parameter (Φ) from the real part of the ac susceptibility according to the following equation27 $$\Phi = \frac{\Delta T_m}{T_m [\Delta \log_{10}(\nu)]}$$ Here, $T_M$ is the temperature corresponding to the observed maximum at the lower-frequency end.27 We used the $\chi'(1$ Hz) and $\chi''(1\text{kHz})$ data to calculate Φ. The calculated value of Φ is 0.072, which is close to the average value of ~0.1 for superparamagnetic systems.26–29 Taken together, these results show that our DIONrods exhibit superparamagnetic behavior. Figure 1. (a) TEM, (b) HRTEM and SAED (inset), (c) XRD, and (d) XPS analyses of DIONrods. Furthermore, we performed experiments to assess the quality and stability of our nanorods in aqueous suspensions. First, the presence of dextran on nanorods’ surface was confirmed by performing clustering experiments with Concanavalin-A (ConA).30,31 Specifically, the presence of dextran on the nanoparticles’ surface can be identified via ConA-induced nanoparticle clustering, due to the strong affinity and multivalency of ConA toward carbohydrates, such as dextran. DLS experiments (Figure 3a) show a time-dependent increase in particle size distribution upon ConA addition, due to formation of nanoparticle assemblies. Most importantly, a fast and reproducible change in T2 relaxation time was observed (Figure 3b), indicating not only the association of dextran with the nanoparticle but also the feasibility of our DIONrods as magnetic relaxation sensors. FT-IR experiments (Figure 3c) further confirmed the presence of characteristic dextran peaks on the DIONrod preparations. Most importantly, the prepared DIONrods can be concentrated in PBS by ultrafiltration, obtaining highly concentrated preparations without nanoparticle precipitation even upon storage at 4 °C for more than 12 months (see Figure 1 in the Supporting Information). Taken together, these results demonstrate the robustness of the dextran coating on the nanorods, making them suitable for biomedical applications. These experiments encouraged us to study the use of our DIONrods as magnetic sensors for bacterial detection. The need for developing fast, sensitive, and reliable detection methods for bacteria in food, the environment, and clinical samples is crucial, in order to safeguard public health. We hypothesized that nanoparticles with larger R2 relaxivity would allow us to use lower amount of nanoparticles (lower amount of iron) and potentially improve the detection limit. We tested our hypothesis using *Mycobacterium avium* spp. *paratuberculosis* (MAP) as our model organism, because the identification of MAP using current methods is difficult due to the bacterium very slow growth. Recently, we reported the detection of MAP in milk with magnetic nanoparticles within 45 min.22 Therefore, we examined if the iron oxide nanorods described herein, having enhanced magnetic properties, can improve the assay’s bacterial detection kinetics. For these experiments, we first cross-linked and aminated the dextran of the iron oxide nanorods to yield aminated DIONrods, before conjugation with anti-MAP antibodies via protein G as previously described.22 The resulting anti-MAP DIONrods (2 μg of Fe per mL) were stable in aqueous solutions and complex media (e.g., PBS, milk) and had an average of 40 antibodies per nanoparticle. This high number of antibodies per particle is due to the larger volume (and therefore surface area) available for binding, which facilitates a larger number of protein G and antibody being conjugated per particle. In our first set of sensing experiments, we used the anti-MAP DIONrods to sense and quantify MAP bacteria in milk. Figure 4a shows a fast response of the anti-MAP DIONrods in the presence of bacteria (100 cfu or colony forming units) in milk, indicated by prominent changes in T2 that plateau at approximately 20 ± 1 ms, 30 min post-bacterial addition. In control experiments, using protein G conjugated DIONrods and no anti-MAP antibodies as well as in saturation experiments where the MAP bacteria was preincubated with the anti-MAP antibodies before sensing, no significant time-dependent increase in T2 was observed. These results to indicate that the fast and sensitive detection of MAP is dependent on the anti-MAP antibody attached to the nanorods. Figure 2. (a) Hysteresis loops obtained at temperatures 5, 100, and 200 K; (b) zero-field-cooled (ZFC) and field-cooled (FC) magnetic susceptibilities in an external magnetic field $H = 200$ G; (c) real and (d) imaginary components of ac susceptibility at different frequencies. These results encouraged us to perform dose-dependent experiments using lower bacterial concentrations and a shorter incubation time. We hypothesized that detection could be obtained within minutes after addition of the DIONrods, due to the nanorods’ high R2 and the high number of antibodies per nanorods. For these experiments, bacterial solutions of MAP were prepared through serial dilutions in milk from an initial stock of 100 cfu of MAP. Afterward, a solution containing the DIONrods (2 μg of Fe per mL) was treated with 20 μL of the corresponding bacterial dilution and incubated for only 5 min at room temperature before measurements were taken. The changes in the T2 relaxation times were monitored with respect to control containing same amount of nanosensor and 20 μL of milk. Results show a linear correlation between ΔT2 and the amount of MAP bacteria in cfu within 5 min of incubation (Figure 4b). Notably, the detection limit (6 cfu) and detection kinetics (5 min) of the assay in milk were improved when the nanorods were used as compared to previous reports with spherical nanoparticles.22 Hence, the observed improvement in bacterial detection using the developed anti-MAP DIONrods can be attributed to both their high magnetic water relaxivity and their large number of antibodies per nanoparticle. One of the most interesting properties of nanomaterials is the enhanced catalytic activities that some of these materials exhibit. Recently, the peroxidase-like activity of iron oxide nanoparticles has been reported.32,33 Therefore, we decided to investigate if our DIONrods, being larger and with a different core geometry (nanorods instead of nanospheres), still possess similar peroxidase-like activity. In these experiments, we incubated DIONrods (2 μg of Fe per mL) with the peroxidase-sensitive dye 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 0.4 mM), which develops a blue color upon oxidation in the presence of hydrogen peroxide and a peroxidase. Results showed that the DIONrods possess peroxidase-like activity as judged by their ability to oxidize the TMB dye in the presence of hydrogen peroxide within 30 min (Figure 5a, vial 2). The observed peroxidase-like activity of our DIONrods is pH-dependent as no oxidation of the dye is observed at pH 7. These results are in line with those recently reported using nanospheres; however, it is worth mentioning that our nanoparticles are bigger, of different shape (nanorods instead of nanospheres), and more magnetic than those previously used. Furthermore, we determined the kinetic parameters of the DIONrods peroxidase-like activity, utilizing TMB as the peroxidase substrate. Specifically, a Michaelis constant, ($K_m$) of 0.5 mM and a maximal reaction velocity ($V_{max}$) of $11.6 \times 10^{−8}$ M/s were obtained from the corresponding LineWeaver–Burk plot (Figure 5b). Notably, the DIONrods’ $V_{max}$ is higher than the ones reported for spherical iron oxide nanoparticles and horseradish peroxidase (20 and 33%, respectively), while having TMB as the peroxidase substrate.32 Furthermore, the DIONrods’ affinity toward TMB is 300 times higher than that of iron oxide nanospheres that have a $K_m$ of ~150 mM.32 Taken together, these data demonstrate the enhanced intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of our DIONrods. Figure 3. (a) Dynamic light scattering study of DIONrods with ConA. (b) Time-dependent response in T2 of DIONrods (200 μL, 0.002 mg of Fe per mL) when treated with 10 μL ConA (1 mg in 1 mL of 1X PBS). (c) FTIR spectra of free dextran and DIONrods. Figure 4. (a) Time-dependent response of anti-MAP DIONrods (200 μL, 0.002 mg of Fe per mL) incubated with 100 cfus of MAP in milk after a 30 min incubation. (b) Dose-dependent responses of MAP at different cfus after a 5 min incubation with anti-MAP DIONrods. MAP was diluted in milk and added to the nanosensors’ solutions (200 μL, containing 0.002 mg of Fe per mL) and changes in T2 were monitored with respect to control containing milk. Figure 5. (a) Peroxidase-like activity of DIONrods. TMB in citrate buffer (pH 4) was treated with H2O2 only as a control (1), with H2O2 and DIONrods (2), and with DIONrods and no H2O2 (3). (b) Kinetics of peroxidase-like activity of DIONrods prepared from high-purity 10-K dextran (Amersham-GE). Finally, we investigated the reproducibility and the role of the dextran’s purity on the nanorods formation. Previous work by Alivisatos and others has shown that the level of purity of the stabilizer or capping agent plays a key role in the geometry of the resulting nanocrytals.34 For the synthesis of our DIONrods, we have used a 10-K dextran polymer (Amersham) with a high degree of purity. Therefore, we examined whether dextrans of lower purity could affect the formation of the nanorods. For these studies, we obtained dextrans of different degrees of purity (Sigma, and Fisher) and used them to synthesize dextran-coated iron oxide nanoparticles. We found that high-purity dextrans reproducibly yield monodisperse rods, whereas dextrans of lower purity (Sigma and Fisher) always yield polydisperse spherical nanoparticles using our procedure (see Figure 2 in the Supporting Information). These results are significant as they point to the dextran level of purity as a key factor in the formation of the DIONrods. It has been reported that dextrans of different molecular weight adopt a particular coil conformation in solutions with distinct dimensions and sizes. For instance, typically a 500-K dextran has a diameter of 29.4 nm (Stokes radius = 14.7 nm), whereas a 10-K dextran is roughly 4.8 nm (Stokes radius ≈ 2.4 nm).35 Hence, through dynamic light scattering (DLS), we determined that the Amersham dextrans had an average diameter of 4.84 nm, with a narrow size distribution (see Figure 2a in the Supporting Information). However, the Sigma and Fisher dextrans had two distinct broad population distributions. Specifically, 87% of the population had an average diameter of 4.8 nm, whereas the remaining 13% had significantly larger size (see Figure 2b in the Supporting Information). Interestingly, this small amount of larger molecular size fractions presumably is enough to affect the shape of the iron oxide nanoparticles (see Figure 2c,d in the Supporting Information). Additionally, the spherical iron oxide nanoparticles obtained with the less pure dextrans displayed poor crystallinity, lower T2 relaxation, and lower peroxidase activity. Specifically, the peroxidase activity of these spherical nanoparticles is lower than that of the DIONrods ($K_m = 1.3$ mM; $V_{max} = 9.8 \times 10^{−8}$ M/s) (see Figure 3 in the Supporting Information). The observed formation of iron oxide nanorods, when a high purity dextran polymer with narrow size distribution and average size of 4.84 nm is used, can be explained by the preferential binding of these polymers to particular surfaces in the initially formed iron oxide crystals. This initiates preferential growth of the nanoparticles in one direction. As the number of higher molecular-weight and therefore larger-sized (>100 nm) dextran impurities increases, they start interfering with this preferential growth process, therefore resulting in a polydisperse spherical iron oxide preparation. ### Conclusion In summary, we have demonstrated a facile, aqueous-based, and room-temperature synthesis of polymer-coated Fe3O4 nanorod. The as-synthesized dextran-coated iron oxide nanorods (DIONrods) exhibit superparamagnetic behavior as well as enhanced water spin–spin relaxation and peroxidase activity. Furthermore, their polymer coating can be easily functionalized with amine groups and conjugated with antibodies for targeting and sensing applications. It is expected that this new type of polymer-coated iron oxide nanorod may find use in many applications including magnetic sensing and diagnostics, hyperthermia and imaging, catalytic oxidations, and the fabrication of devices and nanocomposites with dual magnetic and catalytic activity. **Supporting Information Available:** (1) Photograph of DIONrods aqueous suspension, (2) DLS and TEM of DIONrods prepared with dextran form different ventors, (3) kinetics of peroxidase activity of DIONrods prepared using a dextran of lower purity (PDF).
